channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Nintendo is being sued by customers seeking tariff refunds
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

April 22, 2026

2 Min Read

Logo and image via Nintendo

Nintendo of America is being sued by two customers who claim they are entitled to tariff refunds from the company. 

As noted in a court document surfaced by Aftermath, plaintiffs Gregory Hoffert and Prashant Sharan filed the proposed class action in the United States District Court Western District of Washington on April 21, 2026.

Both claim Nintendo has benefited from controversial tariffs imposed by the United States government after passing the cost onto consumers via price hikes before itself becoming entitled to refunds after the Supreme court decided those same tariffs were unlawful.

"Major U.S. importers—including Nintendo—responded by increasing prices on consumers goods to offset the cost of these tariffs. As a result, American consumers paid higher retail price for consumer goods reflecting the economic burden of those tariffs," reads the filing.

"On February, 20, 2026, the Supreme Court of the United States held that the IEEPA-based tariffs were unlawful. As a consequence of that decision, importers who paid those tariffs—including Nintendo—became entitled to refunds of the duties they previously paid to U.S. Customs and Border Protection.

Related:Dead by Daylight studio Behaviour Interactive confirms layoffs

"[...] Nintendo therefore collected the tariff costs from consumers through elevated pricing, while seeking refunds on the same tariff payments from the federal government."

Hoffert and Sharan allege Nintendo now stands to recover those tariffs payments twice—once from consumers through higher prices, and once again from the federal government. That is, unless the court orders the company to reimburse consumers across America through tariff refunds. 

"Nintendo has made no legally binding commitment to return tariff-related overcharges to the consumers who actually paid them." the filing continues. "This lawsuit seeks to prevent that unjust result."

Hoffert and Sharan claim they are taking action on behalf of "millions of consumers" who purchased goods from Nintendo during the tariff period.

Aftermath has uploaded the complete filing on Scribd.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/nintendo-is-being-sued-by-customers-seeking-tariff-refunds
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Report: Ubisoft cancels social-sim game Alterra
item.content:encoded:
Photograph via Ubisoft's official site

Ubisoft has reportedly canceled Alterra, a social-sim game inspired by the Animal Crossing series.

According to Insider Gaming, the studio reportedly broke the news yesterday, telling employees that the in-development title was canceled before sending staff home for the day.

As Insider Gaming reports, employees working on the game were put on availability for other projects within the company. Game Developer understands the cancellation won't result in job cuts.

Alterra had reportedly been in development for nearly three years at Ubisoft Montreal, and was led by creative director Patrick Redding and lead producer Fabien Lhéraud.

"As part of our portfolio management approach and evolving creative house-led model, we continuously assess projects at every stage of development to ensure alignment with our strategic priorities, quality ambitions, and long-term market potential. Projects that no longer meet these expectations may be discontinued," a Ubisoft representative told Game Developer over email.

Related:Nintendo is being sued by customers seeking tariff refunds

The report comes mere months after Ubisoft announced a global cost-savings plan, which was announced in January of this year. Since then, the company ended game development at Red Storm and laid off 105 workers, and closed its Halifax studio weeks after workers voted to unionize. Then, the publisher confirmed that it quietly made 29 people redundant at its studio in Abu Dhabi in November 2025. In February, Ubisoft Toronto laid off 40 employees.

Approximately 1,200 workers, in turn, responded by striking on February 10. Days prior, two union representatives demanded the resignation of Ubisoft CEO Yves Guillemot. Meanwhile, a three-day international strike was called by five unions in France to demand leadership take "responsibility for the consequences of their catastrophic management."

Earlier this month, the unionized workers impacted by the closure of Ubisoft Halifax voted in favor of a settlement with the company. The terms of the settlement, including the compensation employees will receive, is confidential. Former Ubisoft Halifax employee Jon Huffman said the decision shocked all 71 employees at the studio, but thanked CWA Canada for helping to negotiate a settlement.

"It came as a surprise to all of us and to the front-line managers as we were in the middle of many on-going and new projects, and doing great work," said Huffman. "We are extremely grateful to our union for negotiating this package for us."

At the time of writing, Ubisoft has yet to publicly acknowledge the reported project. According to the report, Alterra featured a voxel art style and mechanics similar to Minecraft, where players could buy an array of objects with gathered materials. Images seen in the past by Insider Game reportedly showed Matterlings, the NPCs in the game, looking similar to Funko Pop characters. The world consisted of multiple biomes with different creatures and materials to gather, fight, and meet.

Related:Dead by Daylight studio Behaviour Interactive confirms layoffs

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/report-ubisoft-cancels-social-sim-game-alterra
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
